In this work, we conduct entropy-based pilot experiments and observe a strong positive correlation between entropy reduction and high-quality tool calls. Building on this finding, we propose using entropy reduction as a supervisory signal and design two reward strategies to address the differing needs of optimizing tool-use behavior. Sparse outcome rewards provide coarse, trajectory-level guidance to improve efficiency, while dense process rewards offer fine-grained supervision to enhance performance. Experiments across diverse domains show that both reward designs improve tool-use behavior: the former reduces tool calls by 72.07% compared to the average of baselines, while the latter improves performance by 22.27%. These results position entropy reduction as a key mechanism for enhancing tool-use behavior, enabling agents to be more adaptive in real-world applications.
title: Rethinking the Role of Entropy in Optimizing Tool-Use Behaviors for Large Language Model Agents

abstract: Language models (LMs) are becoming increasingly dependent on external tools. LM-based agentic frameworks frequently interact with their environment via such tools to search files, run code, call APIs, etc. Further, modern reasoning-based LMs use tools such as web search and Python code execution to enhance their reasoning capabilities. While tools greatly improve the capabilities of LMs, they also introduce performance bottlenecks during the inference process. In this paper, we introduce novel systems optimizations to address such performance bottlenecks by speculating tool calls and forcing sequences to remain resident in the inference engine to minimize overheads. Our optimizations lead to throughput improvements of several hundred tokens per second when hosting inference for LM agents. We provide a theoretical analysis of our algorithms to provide insights into speculation configurations that will yield the best performance. Further, we recommend a new "tool cache" API endpoint to enable LM providers to easily adopt these optimizations.

keyInsights: Speculating stateless and 'cheap' tools effectively masks their latency, with optimal gains occurring when tool execution time approximately equals the main model's generation time.
Integrating tool caching directly into the inference engine by keeping sequences resident and injecting tool outputs/arguments further reduces overheads beyond client-side approaches.
The accuracy of smaller speculative models can be significantly boosted by increasing the number of speculative samples (λ), allowing them to approach the performance of larger models.
originalProblem: Agentic LLMs using external tools face significant performance bottlenecks due to sequential dependencies and unpredictable tool execution latencies.
Traditional LLM inference engines incur substantial overheads (KV-cache eviction, re-scheduling, re-prefilling) when tool calls interrupt the generation process.
Existing LLM optimizations like prefix caching and speculative decoding are not adequately designed for the long, variable latencies of external tool calls.
results: Client-side speculation achieved 6-21% end-to-end time savings for agents and throughput improvements of up to 196 tokens per second.

Engine-side speculation provided an additional 2-3% time savings over client-side methods, most notably for tool latencies between 0 and 1 second, by avoiding sequence eviction and enabling early decoding.
For commercial APIs, client-side speculation yielded nearly 10% time savings per 100 turns for a ~4% increase in cost, demonstrating practical applicability.
solution: Introduce speculative tool execution to anticipate and initiate tool calls before the main LLM explicitly requests them, thereby overlapping tool latency with LM generation.
Develop a client-side speculative tool calling approach that asynchronously executes tools predicted by a smaller, faster model and caches results for the main LM to consume.

Implement an engine-side speculative tool calling approach that integrates tool caching directly into the inference engine, allowing the LLM's sequence to remain resident and leveraging 'early exit decoding' to inject tool arguments and outputs.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory and the University of Maryland introduce speculative tool calling to enhance agentic LLM inference performance by overlapping tool execution with model generation. The approach delivers 6-21% end-to-end time savings via client-side speculation and an additional 2-3% with engine-side integration, while improving throughput by hundreds of tokens per second.

title: Optimizing Agentic Language Model Inference via Speculative Tool Calls

abstract: Recent advancements in integrating large language models (LLMs) with tools have allowed the models to interact with real-world environments. However, these tool-augmented LLMs often encounter incomplete scenarios when users provide partial information or the necessary tools are unavailable. Recognizing and managing such scenarios is crucial for LLMs to ensure their reliability, but this exploration remains understudied. This study examines whether LLMs can identify incomplete conditions and appropriately determine when to refrain from using tools. To quantitatively evaluate this capability, we construct a new benchmark dataset where instances are systematically altered to simulate the ambiguous and incomplete conditions common in real-world interactions. Our experiments reveal that even state-of-the-art LLMs often struggle to identify these conditions, attempting to use tools without sufficient information or when the correct tool is unavailable. To better understand these limitations, we conduct a detailed behavioral analysis across various conditions, including implicit evaluation and scenarios where models receive feedback from previous tool invocations. Based on this analysis, we propose a novel prompting-based reasoning strategy that explicitly instructs models to assess the sufficiency of information and the availability of tools. Our proposed approach significantly enhances the models' ability to recognize incomplete conditions, resulting in more informed and contextually appropriate tool-use decisions. We believe our research contributes to advancing the reliability of LLMs, especially in real-world applications where incomplete or ambiguous information is common. Our dataset is available at this https URL.
title: Can Tool-augmented Large Language Models be Aware of Incomplete Conditions?

abstract: Agentic AI enables LLM to dynamically reason, plan, and interact with tools to solve complex tasks. However, agentic workflows often require many iterative reasoning steps and tool invocations, leading to significant operational expense, end-to-end latency and failures due to hallucinations. This work introduces Agent Workflow Optimization (AWO), a framework that identifies and optimizes redundant tool execution patterns to improve the efficiency and robustness of agentic workflows. AWO analyzes existing workflow traces to discover recurring sequences of tool calls and transforms them into meta-tools, which are deterministic, composite tools that bundle multiple agent actions into a single invocation. Meta-tools bypass unnecessary intermediate LLM reasoning steps and reduce operational cost while also shortening execution paths, leading to fewer failures. Experiments on two agentic AI benchmarks show that AWO reduces the number of LLM calls up to 11.9% while also increasing the task success rate by up to 4.2 percent points.

keyInsights: Despite their flexibility, agentic workflows exhibit highly regular and repetitive tool-invocation patterns, especially in their initial stages.
Principles from traditional compiler optimizations, such as function inlining and kernel fusion, can be effectively adapted to optimize non-deterministic LLM agent workflows.
Encapsulating common multi-step agent behaviors into deterministic meta-tools significantly reduces the agent's cognitive load and the number of explicit LLM decisions required.
originalProblem: Agentic AI systems incur high operational costs and latency due to numerous iterative LLM reasoning steps and tool invocations.
The non-determinism of LLMs can lead agents to execute redundant or suboptimal action sequences, further increasing computational overhead.
Current inefficiencies pose significant barriers to the scalability and widespread adoption of agentic systems in production environments.
results: Achieved a reduction in LLM calls of 5.6% to 11.9% and operational costs of 4.2% to 15% across various LLMs and benchmarks.
Improved task success rates by up to 4.2 percentage points on VISUALWEBARENA and 3.6 percentage points on APPWORLD, demonstrating enhanced robustness.
Meta-tools shortened average task trajectory length by 1.0 to 2.63 steps, with utilization rates reaching 98.2% in highly repetitive scenarios.
solution: AWO analyzes historical agent execution traces by constructing a state graph that represents sequences of tool calls.
It employs domain-specific 'horizontal merging' rules to identify and combine semantically equivalent states, exposing recurring patterns across different executions.
A 'vertical merging' algorithm extracts frequent, multi-step tool call sequences from the merged graph, compiling them into single, callable 'meta-tools' for the agent.
paper_summary.summary: The Agent Workflow Optimization (AWO) framework streamlines agentic AI systems by converting common, repetitive tool execution patterns into deterministic "meta-tools". This approach reduces the number of LLM calls, token usage, and operational costs while generally improving task success rates.

title: Optimizing Agentic Workflows using Meta-tools

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are demonstrating rapid improvements on complex reasoning benchmarks, particularly when allowed to utilize intermediate reasoning steps before converging on a final solution. However, current literature often overlooks the significant computational burden associated with generating long reasoning sequences. For industrial applications, model selection depends not only on raw accuracy but also on resource constraints and inference costs. In this work, we conduct a test-time-compute aware evaluation of both contemporary and older open-source LLMs, mapping their Pareto frontiers across math- and reasoning-intensive benchmarks. Our findings identify the Mixture of Experts (MoE) architecture as a strong candidate to balance performance and efficiency in our evaluation setting. Furthermore, we trace the trajectory of Pareto efficiency over time to derive an emergent trend regarding accuracy gain per unit of compute. Finally, we demonstrate that there is a saturation point for inference-time compute. Beyond a certain threshold, accuracy gains diminish, indicating that while extended reasoning capabilities are beneficial, they cannot overcome intrinsic model limitations regarding specific complexities.

organization_info.name: Budapest University of Technology and Economics

keyInsights: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures consistently outperform dense models, dominating the compute-accuracy Pareto frontiers due to their ability to generate longer reasoning chains within comparable computational budgets.

Incorrect reasoning traces consume substantially more compute than correct ones in most models (up to 97%), revealing a significant inefficiency where LLMs engage in prolonged, futile attempts at problem resolution.
Smaller models can achieve Pareto-optimal accuracy points by extending inference-time reasoning, demonstrating that "thinking compute" can compensate for limited architectural capacity.
originalProblem: Academic focus on maximizing LLM accuracy often disregards the significant computational costs, latency, and hardware constraints in real-world industrial deployment.
Existing LLM evaluations frequently assume unconstrained inference-time compute, especially for long Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning, leading to an incomplete understanding of true model efficiency.
Practitioners lack a clear, multi-dimensional framework to select and deploy open-source LLMs that effectively balance reasoning performance with computational expenditure.
results: Established a consistent piecewise-linear relationship between inference compute and task accuracy, characterized by initial rapid gains followed by task-dependent saturation points (Pareto knees).

Showed a general upward trend in "Efficiency Score" (mean accuracy / log average FLOPs per query) from mid-2023 to mid-2025, indicating improvements in reasoning capability relative to computational cost, particularly for specialized models.

Discovered that for challenging benchmarks (e.g., GPQA-Diamond, AIME), MoE models allow for significantly longer reasoning at comparable FLOPs, offering a superior conversion of computational resources to reasoning accuracy.
solution: Implemented a rigorous, compute-aware evaluation methodology centered on a single-pass Chain-of-Thought (CoT) decoding protocol across five diverse math and reasoning benchmarks.
Evaluated 19 state-of-the-art open-source LLMs, encompassing both dense and Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures, across a broad range of parameter scales.

Developed a component-aware FLOPs estimator that precisely quantifies inference compute by accounting for specific architectural features like attention projections, FFN types, MoE expert activation, and Mamba layers.
paper_summary.summary: Prucs et al. map compute-accuracy Pareto frontiers for open-source Large Language Models (LLMs) on reasoning tasks, revealing that Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures offer superior efficiency by consistently dominating these frontiers. Their analysis quantifies the substantial computational cost of incorrect reasoning traces and demonstrates that smaller models can achieve high accuracy by leveraging extended inference-time computation, providing a practical framework for resource-aware LLM deployment.

title: Compute-Accuracy Pareto Frontiers for Open-Source Reasoning Large Language Models

abstract:
While integrating external tools into large language models (LLMs) enhances
their ability to access real-time information and domain-specific services,
existing approaches focus narrowly on functional tool selection following user
instructions, overlooking the context-aware personalization in tool selection.
This oversight leads to suboptimal user satisfaction and inefficient tool
utilization, particularly when overlapping toolsets require nuanced selection
based on contextual factors. To bridge this gap, we introduce ToolSpectrum, a
benchmark designed to evaluate LLMs' capabilities in personalized tool
utilization. Specifically, we formalize two key dimensions of personalization,
user profile and environmental factors, and analyze their individual and
synergistic impacts on tool utilization. Through extensive experiments on
ToolSpectrum, we demonstrate that personalized tool utilization significantly
improves user experience across diverse scenarios. However, even
state-of-the-art LLMs exhibit the limited ability to reason jointly about user
profiles and environmental factors, often prioritizing one dimension at the
expense of the other. Our findings underscore the necessity of context-aware
personalization in tool-augmented LLMs and reveal critical limitations for
current models. Our data and code are available at
this https URL

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
University of Science and Technology Beijing

keyInsights: Incorporating personalized factors (user profiles, environmental contexts) substantially improves tool utilization effectiveness and user experience, even for state-of-the-art LLMs.
Current LLMs face challenges in fine-grained personalization, particularly in correctly generating optional parameters that align with nuanced contextual cues.

Models exhibit reduced performance when required to reason jointly about multiple personalized factors (e.g., combining user profiles and environmental conditions), highlighting difficulty in complex context integration.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-tool integration benchmarks primarily focus on functional tool selection based on user instructions, overlooking context-aware personalization.
Current approaches do not account for scenarios with overlapping tool functionalities where the optimal tool depends on specific user profiles and environmental factors.
The lack of context-aware personalization leads to suboptimal user satisfaction, inefficient tool utilization, and an inability to adhere to domain-specific policies.
results: API-based models, such as GPT-4o, generally outperform open-source models, but even top performers achieved only an F1 score of 0.50 for optional parameters in combined profile and environment scenarios.

Error analysis on GPT-4o revealed that 37% of errors were due to insufficient understanding of personalized features for optional parameter generation, and 31% stemmed from inadequate comprehension of interactions between profiles and environments, particularly concerning domain policies.

Prompting strategies like Chain-of-Thought and hierarchical prompting consistently improved performance, with hierarchical prompting demonstrating notable gains by reducing context noise and enhancing LLMs' ability to process personalized information.
solution: Formalized "personalized tool utilization" as a function mapping user instructions, profiles, environments, and toolsets to a context-aware tool call.

Developed ToolSpectrum, a novel benchmark with a meticulously curated toolset, diverse user profiles, and comprehensive environmental factors (natural, digital, and domain policies) across 9 application domains.
Employed a three-stage data collection pipeline using GPT-4o for generation, followed by rigorous human and automated quality control to ensure data consistency and accuracy.
paper_summary.summary: This paper introduces ToolSpectrum, a new benchmark designed to evaluate Large Language Models' capacity for personalized tool utilization by considering user profiles and environmental factors. Experiments reveal that while personalization improves outcomes, current LLMs struggle with fine-grained context integration, particularly in selecting optional parameters and reasoning across multiple interacting contextual dimensions.

title: ToolSpectrum : Towards Personalized Tool Utilization for Large Language  Models

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable performance on long-context tasks, but are often bottlenecked by memory constraints. Namely, the KV cache, which is used to significantly speed up attention computations, grows linearly with context length. A suite of compression algorithms has been introduced to alleviate cache growth by evicting unimportant tokens. However, several popular strategies are targeted towards the prefill phase, i.e., processing long prompt context, and their performance is rarely assessed on reasoning tasks requiring long decoding. In particular, short but complex prompts, such as those in benchmarks like GSM8K and MATH500, often benefit from multi-step reasoning and self-reflection, resulting in thinking sequences thousands of tokens long. In this work, we benchmark the performance of several popular compression strategies on long-reasoning tasks. For the non-reasoning Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, we determine that no singular strategy fits all, and that performance is heavily influenced by dataset type. However, we discover that H2O and our decoding-enabled variant of SnapKV are dominant strategies for reasoning models, indicating the utility of heavy-hitter tracking for reasoning traces. We also find that eviction strategies at low budgets can produce longer reasoning traces, revealing a tradeoff between cache size and inference costs.

keyInsights: Attention-based 'heavy-hitter' strategies like H2O and SnapKV-Decoding are particularly effective for reasoning models due to the persistent importance of certain tokens and critical keywords in multi-step thought processes.

Aggressive KV cache compression can lead to a counter-intuitive trade-off, where smaller cache budgets may cause models to generate longer, sometimes non-terminating, reasoning traces, thereby increasing total inference time and cost.

A strong correlation exists between lower attention loss (preserving the attention distribution after eviction) and higher model performance, indicating the importance of maintaining attention fidelity.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) performing multi-step reasoning generate long intermediate sequences (Chain-of-Thought), creating a significant memory bottleneck due to the linearly growing Key-Value (KV) cache.
Existing KV cache compression evaluations primarily focus on long input prompts, overlooking the challenges of long *generation* sequences that dominate memory consumption in reasoning tasks.
A lack of empirical understanding regarding which KV cache compression methods effectively preserve LLM reasoning capabilities under memory constraints.
results: H2O and the newly adapted SnapKV-Decoding (SnapKV-D) consistently and significantly outperformed all other tested compression methods for specialized reasoning models across diverse datasets and cache budgets.

Lower cache budgets often resulted in paradoxically longer generation outputs for reasoning models, with less effective compression strategies sometimes leading to non-terminating outputs or 'circular babble'.

SnapKV-D and H2O demonstrated the least attention loss and highest retention rate of critical keywords in the KV cache, empirically validating their superior performance in preserving reasoning quality.
solution: A comprehensive benchmark was conducted on six state-of-the-art KV cache compression methods (H2O, StreamingLLM, R-KV, KNorm, ShadowKV, and a novel decoding-enabled SnapKV) across eight diverse reasoning benchmarks.

Evaluations used a mix of instruction-tuned and specialized reasoning LLMs (e.g., Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, DeepSeek-R1, Nemotron-Nano-8B-v1) under realistic single-GPU constraints, assessing performance across various cache budgets and generation limits.
The NVIDIA `kvpress` library was forked and modified to support decoding-phase compression and additional methods, with the intent of open-sourcing for community use.
paper_summary.summary: This research evaluates KV cache compression strategies for large language models engaged in long-form reasoning, finding that attention-based heavy-hitter methods, particularly H2O and SnapKV-Decoding, consistently yield superior performance for reasoning models. The study also uncovers a critical trade-off where aggressive memory compression can paradoxically increase total inference costs by extending generation lengths.

title: Hold Onto That Thought: Assessing KV Cache Compression On Reasoning

abstract:
Large Language Models (LLMs) encounter severe memory inefficiencies during
long-context inference due to conventional handling of key-value (KV) caches.
In this work, we introduce a novel integration of PagedAttention with PyTorch's
FlexAttention, addressing internal fragmentation and inefficiencies associated
with monolithic KV cache allocations. Implemented within IBM's Foundation Model
Stack (FMS), our fused attention kernel efficiently gathers scattered KV data.
Our benchmarks on an NVIDIA L4 GPU (24GB) demonstrate significantly reduced
inference latency, growing only linearly (~2x) with sequence length from 128 to
2048 tokens when utilizing a global KV cache, compared to exponential latency
increases without caching. While peak memory usage remains largely unchanged
for single-step evaluations (dominated by model weights and activations), paged
attention causes minimal incremental memory usage, observable only at sequence
lengths exceeding 2048 tokens due to its power-of-two cache allocations. We
open-source the full implementation and discuss its implications for future
long-context model deployment.
title: Paged Attention Meets FlexAttention: Unlocking Long-Context Efficiency  in Deployed Inference

abstract: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) have achieved remarkable performance on complex tasks by engaging in extended reasoning before producing final answers, yet this strength introduces the risk of overthinking, where excessive token generation occurs even for simple tasks. While recent work in efficient reasoning seeks to reduce reasoning length while preserving accuracy, it remains unclear whether such optimization is truly a free lunch. Drawing on the intuition that compressing reasoning may reduce the robustness of model responses and lead models to omit key reasoning steps, we investigate whether efficient reasoning strategies introduce behavioral inconsistencies. To systematically assess this, we introduce $ICBENCH$, a benchmark designed to measure inconsistency in LRMs across three dimensions: inconsistency across task settings (ITS), inconsistency between training objectives and learned behavior (TR-LB), and inconsistency between internal reasoning and self-explanations (IR-SE). Applying $ICBENCH$ to a range of open-source LRMs, we find that while larger models generally exhibit greater consistency than smaller ones, they all display widespread "scheming" behaviors, including self-disagreement, post-hoc rationalization, and the withholding of reasoning cues. Crucially, our results demonstrate that efficient reasoning strategies such as No-Thinking and Simple Token-Budget consistently increase all three defined types of inconsistency. These findings suggest that although efficient reasoning enhances token-level efficiency, further investigation is imperative to ascertain whether it concurrently introduces the risk of models evading effective supervision.

author_info.institution: King Abdullah University of Science and Technology

organization_info.name: King Abdullah University of Science and Technology
Provable Responsible AI and Data Analytics (PRADA) Lab

keyInsights: Training-free efficient reasoning strategies (e.g., NoThinking, Simple Token-Budget) consistently increase behavioral inconsistency in LRMs across various settings.
Large Reasoning Models can exhibit "scheming" behaviors such as self-disagreement, post-hoc rationalization of answers, and withholding critical influencing factors.
While model scaling generally improves LRM consistency, this improvement does not consistently extend to alignment or ethical tasks, indicating more complex challenges.
originalProblem: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) often "overthink," generating excessive tokens and incurring high computational costs and latency.
Existing efficient reasoning strategies primarily focus on maintaining accuracy, implicitly assuming efficiency gains come without other behavioral costs.
There was no systematic framework to investigate how efficiency optimization might introduce or exacerbate various forms of behavioral inconsistencies in LRMs.
results: Larger LRM variants (e.g., DeepSeek-R1 14B, Qwen-3 14B) generally showed lower Inconsistency across Task Settings (ITS) and Inconsistency between Training Objectives and Learned Model Behavior (TR-LB) scores.
Training-free efficient reasoning strategies like "NoThinking" and "Simple Token-Budget" substantially increased all three types of behavioral inconsistency (ITS, TR-LB, IR-SE) across evaluated LRMs.
Under token budget constraints, models showed a reduced "thinking ratio" and an increased tendency for "withholding influencing factors," obscuring their true reasoning.
solution: Three types of behavioral inconsistency in LRMs were systematically defined: Inconsistency across Task Settings (ITS), Inconsistency between Training Objectives and Learned Model Behavior (TR-LB), and Inconsistency between Internal Reasoning and Output Self-Explanations (IR-SE).
A new benchmark, ICBENCH, was developed to measure these inconsistencies using re-formatted mathematical reasoning and alignment tasks with adversarial inputs.
Open-source LRMs (DeepSeek-R1, Qwen-3) were evaluated under standard, "NoThinking," and "Simple Token-Budget" reasoning settings to assess the impact of efficiency strategies.
paper_summary.summary: Investigating Large Reasoning Models (LRMs), this research reveals that training-free efficient reasoning strategies degrade model consistency and can induce "scheming" behaviors like post-hoc rationalization. The study develops ICBENCH, a new benchmark, to systematically measure these behavioral inconsistencies across different task settings and internal reasoning processes.

title: Is Long-to-Short a Free Lunch? Investigating Inconsistency and Reasoning Efficiency in LRMs

abstract: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) have demonstrated strong performance by producing extended Chain-of-Thought (CoT) traces before answering. However, this paradigm often induces over-reasoning: redundant calculations and circular self-verification that increase computational cost without improving outcomes. Existing evaluations largely emphasize final accuracy or coarse token counts, and lack automated tools to separate essential logic from structural redundancy. We introduce CoTJudger, a graph-driven framework that quantifies reasoning efficiency by converting free-form CoTs into directed dependency graphs and extracting the Shortest Effective Path (SEP) needed to reach a correct solution. This yields an interpretable efficiency signal -- how much of a CoT is necessary versus structurally redundant -- that is comparable across models and tasks. Evaluating 21 LRMs, CoTJudger reveals pervasive redundancy and surfaces recurring failure modes, including verification obsession and compensatory redundancy. These results provide a practical metric for disentangling reasoning ability from computational waste, enabling more targeted evaluation and diagnosis of LRM efficiency.

keyInsights: Redundancy is widespread across LRMs but manifests in distinct topological pathologies, such as cyclic complexity, semantic verbosity, or localized micro-inefficiencies.

Distilled LRMs tend to inherit and amplify structural redundancy from teacher models, leading to "distillation bloat" and surface-level imitation of reflection without stable verification capabilities.

CoT redundancy serves a functional role in context maintenance and stability during mid-stage reasoning, rather than merely being random noise, and accumulates significantly towards the end of a trace for self-checking.
originalProblem: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) using Chain-of-Thought (CoT) often produce "over-reasoning" characterized by redundant computations, unproductive backtracking, and circular self-verification.

Existing CoT evaluation methods primarily rely on coarse metrics like token counts or resource-intensive human annotations, which fail to distinguish essential logical complexity from structural waste.
There is a lack of automated, structural frameworks capable of diagnosing and quantifying the underlying sources of inefficiency within CoT reasoning.
results: CoTJudger revealed a significant "efficiency gap," with models like Qwen3-Max allocating over 80% of their inference budget to non-essential steps (Redundancy Ratio = 86.5%).

Topological analysis identified distinct redundancy patterns: DeepSeek-R1 showed cyclic complexity (average degree up to 1.96), while Qwen3-Max exhibited semantic verbosity (uncertainty ratio of 0.955, 10.5% isolated nodes).

Proprietary models generally maintained stable, linear reasoning, whereas open-source and distilled models displayed elevated redundancy, particularly accumulating in later steps and exhibiting "reasoning illusions" in post-answer verification.
solution: CoTJudger, a graph-driven framework, converts free-form CoT traces into directed dependency graphs using LLMs for atomization, classification, and validation.
It algorithmically extracts the Shortest Effective Path (SEP), representing the minimal, logically consistent reasoning sequence required to reach a correct answer.
The framework calculates a suite of topological and efficiency metrics, including the Redundancy Ratio, to precisely quantify and diagnose over-reasoning patterns.
paper_summary.summary: CoTJudger introduces an automated, graph-driven framework to evaluate the efficiency and redundancy of Chain-of-Thought traces in Large Reasoning Models. It quantifies the gap between allocated computation and logical necessity, identifying specific topological patterns of over-reasoning across various models and tasks.

title: CoTJudger: A Graph-Driven Framework for Automatic Evaluation of Chain-of-Thought Efficiency and Redundancy in LRMs

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have recently been applied to reranking tasks in information retrieval, achieving strong performance. However, their high computational demands often hinder practical deployment. Existing studies evaluate the efficiency of LLM-based rerankers using proxy metrics such as latency, the number of forward passes, input tokens, and output tokens. However, these metrics depend on hardware and running-time choices (\eg parallel or not, batch size, etc), and often fail to account for model size, making it difficult to interpret and obscuring the evaluation of the efficiency-effectiveness tradeoff. To address this issue, we propose \ours\footnote{this https URL.} for LLM-based rerankers: RPP (ranking metrics per PetaFLOP), measuring how much ranking quality (e.g., NDCG or MRR) a method achieves per PetaFLOP, and QPP (queries per PetaFLOP), measuring how many queries can be processed per PetaFLOP. Accompanied by the new metrics, an interpretable FLOPs estimator is developed to estimate the FLOPs of an LLM-based reranker even without running any experiments. Based on the proposed metrics, we conduct comprehensive experiments to evaluate a wide range of LLM-based rerankers with different architectures, studying the efficiency-effectiveness trade-off and bringing this issue to the attention of the research community.
title: Efficiency-Effectiveness Reranking FLOPs for LLM-based Rerankers

abstract: Large language models trained for reasoning trade off inference tokens against accuracy, yet standard evaluations report only final accuracy, obscuring where tokens are spent or wasted. We introduce a trace-optional framework that decomposes token efficiency into interpretable factors: completion under a fixed token budget (avoiding truncation), conditional correctness given completion, and verbosity (token usage). When benchmark metadata provides per-instance workload proxies, we further factor verbosity into two components: mean verbalization overhead (tokens per work unit) and a coupling coefficient capturing how overhead scales with task workload. When reasoning traces are available, we add deterministic trace-quality measures (grounding, repetition, prompt copying) to separate degenerate looping from verbose-but-engaged reasoning, avoiding human labeling and LLM judges. Evaluating 25 models on CogniLoad, we find that accuracy and token-efficiency rankings diverge (Spearman $\rho=0.63$), efficiency gaps are often driven by conditional correctness, and verbalization overhead varies by about 9 times (only weakly related to model scale). Our decomposition reveals distinct bottleneck profiles that suggest different efficiency interventions.

keyInsights: Efficiency (cost-effectiveness, measured as correct answers per token) and accuracy are distinct metrics for LLMs, with model rankings often diverging, indicating that high accuracy does not guarantee high efficiency.

LLM reasoning inefficiencies are attributable to distinct, quantifiable failure modes (e.g., logical errors, context limits, excessive verbalization), which require different diagnostic approaches for targeted intervention.

Lightweight, deterministic trace-quality metrics, based on surface statistics like token grounding and repetition, can effectively distinguish productive but verbose reasoning from degenerate or repetitive outputs without relying on costly human or LLM-based judgments.
originalProblem: Current LLM benchmarks primarily report final accuracy, obscuring how computational resources (tokens) are spent or wasted during the reasoning process.

Existing efficiency metrics are either too aggregated (conflating distinct failure modes), too costly (relying on human or LLM-as-a-judge for trace analysis), or fail to normalize token usage by the intrinsic complexity of a given task.

The 'overthinking' phenomenon shows that extended Chain-of-Thought reasoning can sometimes degrade performance through 'hallucinated doubt' and error propagation, highlighting the need to distinguish productive token usage from wasteful verbosity.
results: A moderate Spearman correlation (ρ = 0.63) was found between token efficiency (E0) and raw accuracy across 25 models, confirming that these metrics yield different model rankings.

Efficiency differences were primarily driven by logic robustness (r_logic) for many open-weight models (indicating conciseness but errors), while context truncation (r_ctx) was a significant bottleneck for budget-limited models (e.g., Gemini-2.5-Pro).

Workload-normalized verbosity (overline{VO}) varied by approximately 9x across models and was weakly related to model scale; models consistently exhibited sublinear scaling (κ from 0.24 to 0.60), compressing reasoning on complex tasks, while trace quality (q_trace) varied by an order of magnitude, identifying models with degenerate looping versus engaged but verbose reasoning.
solution: A three-level trace-optional framework is developed to decompose token efficiency, structured to provide increasingly granular and interpretable factors based on available data (outcomes, benchmark metadata, or reasoning traces).
Level 1 decomposes outcome-level efficiency into robustness against token budget truncation (r_ctx), logic robustness (r_logic), and overall verbosity (expected token usage).

Level 2 refines verbosity by normalizing token usage against the intrinsic task complexity using workload proxies (yielding verbalization overhead and coupling coefficient), while Level 3 further breaks down verbosity into a trace quality factor and signal-specific verbalization using deterministic, surface-level statistics to avoid expensive annotations.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from UiT The Arctic University of Norway and the University of Oslo introduce a trace-optional framework for decomposing large language model reasoning efficiency into distinct factors like context robustness, logic robustness, and verbalization overhead. This framework reveals that efficiency and accuracy rankings diverge and identifies specific bottlenecks—such as logical errors or context limitations—that standard aggregated metrics obscure.

title: Decomposing Reasoning Efficiency in Large Language Models

abstract: Serving large language models (LLMs) in production can incur substantial costs, which has prompted recent advances in inference system optimizations. Today, these systems are evaluated against conventional latency and throughput metrics (eg. TTFT, TBT, Normalised Latency and TPOT). However, these metrics fail to fully capture the nuances of LLM inference, leading to an incomplete assessment of user-facing performance crucial for real-time applications such as chat and translation. In this paper, we first identify the pitfalls of current performance metrics in evaluating LLM inference systems. We then propose Etalon, a comprehensive performance evaluation framework that includes fluidity-index -- a novel metric designed to reflect the intricacies of the LLM inference process and its impact on real-time user experience. Finally, we evaluate various existing open-source platforms and model-as-a-service offerings using Etalon, discussing their strengths and weaknesses. Etalon is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Conventional LLM inference metrics like Time Per Output Token (TPOT) can significantly overestimate system throughput by masking intermittent stalls and jitters that severely degrade user experience.

The `fluidity-index` offers a more accurate, user-centric perspective, revealing that systems with high average throughput might exhibit poor fluidity, while others excel in consistent, smooth token delivery.

Prefill-decode interference and the unique behaviors of modern techniques like speculative decoding or chunked prefill significantly impact perceived fluidity, which is not adequately captured by simple average or tail latency metrics.
originalProblem: Existing LLM inference performance metrics (e.g., Time To First Token, Time Between Tokens, Time Per Output Token) incompletely capture the holistic user experience, especially in real-time, streaming interactions.

Conventional aggregate or averaged metrics fail to expose critical delays, intermittent jitters, or the dynamic interplay between prefill and decode phases, leading to misleading performance assessments.
A lack of standardized, user-centric evaluation methods hinders fair black-box comparison of proprietary Model-as-a-Service (MaaS) offerings and effective capacity planning for open-source systems.
results: Black-box evaluation of public APIs showed Groq achieved the highest TPOT (600 tokens/s) but had significant jitters, with the `fluid token generation rate` providing a more balanced throughput view aligned with user perception.
Fireworks consistently demonstrated the lowest Time To First Token (TTFT) and variance across prompt lengths, indicating efficient prompt processing compared to Anyscale and Groq.

Capacity evaluation using `fluidity-index` SLOs revealed that Sarathi-Serve (with chunked prefill) maintained higher overall fluidity and fewer deadline misses than vLLM, particularly when operating at its determined maximum sustainable request load.
solution: Etalon, an open-source framework, introduces the `fluidity-index` metric, inspired by deadline-based evaluation in real-time systems and media streaming.

The `fluidity-index` evaluates token generation consistency by establishing dynamic prefill (`D_p`) and decode (`D_d`) deadlines for each token, accumulating 'slack' for early arrivals, and resetting deadlines upon misses to reflect system recovery.

It defines a `fluid token generation rate` to determine the maximum sustainable playback rate under specified fluidity constraints, integrated into practical black-box and capacity evaluation workflows.
paper_summary.summary: Etalon introduces a framework and `fluidity-index` metric to holistically evaluate Large Language Model inference systems by measuring the temporal consistency of token generation. Its application demonstrated that conventional metrics often obscure critical jitters and stalls, while `fluidity-index` provides a more user-centric view, revealing true sustainable throughput and highlighting performance differences among services like Anyscale, Groq, and Fireworks. The framework is open-sourced by researchers from Georgia Institute of Technology, Microsoft Research India, and Intel AI Lab.

title: Etalon: Holistic Performance Evaluation Framework for LLM Inference Systems

abstract:
Large reasoning models (LRMs) have achieved impressive performance in complex
tasks, often outperforming conventional large language models (LLMs). However,
the prevalent issue of overthinking severely limits their computational
efficiency. Overthinking occurs when models generate excessive and redundant
tokens that contribute little to accurate outcomes, especially in simple tasks,
resulting in a significant waste of computational resources. To systematically
investigate this issue, we introduce Think-Bench, a benchmark designed to
evaluate the reasoning efficiency of LRMs. We also propose novel efficiency
metrics and conduct a comprehensive evaluation of various LRMs across multiple
dimensions, including the reasoning process, outcome quality, and
chain-of-thought (CoT) characteristics. Our analysis reveals that most LRMs
exhibit overthinking in handling easy questions, generating unnecessarily
lengthy reasoning chains. While many LRMs demonstrate high CoT quality, several
suffer from low efficiency. We hope that Think-Bench can serve as a robust
foundation for advancing research into LRMs.

keyInsights: Most large reasoning models, including advanced ones, commonly exhibit "overthinking," generating unnecessarily verbose reasoning even for straightforward problems, leading to significant computational overhead.

There is a consistent trade-off between reasoning efficiency (e.g., token usage) and CoT quality (e.g., recall and precision), where models achieving higher quality often do so at the cost of lower efficiency.

Models demonstrate varying reflective behaviors, with some exhibiting frequent path changes ("Thought Num") that do not necessarily correlate with improved efficiency or quality, while others maintain stable, direct reasoning processes.
originalProblem: Existing large language model evaluation benchmarks primarily focus on final answer accuracy, neglecting the quality and efficiency of intermediate reasoning steps.

Large reasoning models (LRMs) often engage in "overthinking," generating excessively long and redundant reasoning chains that waste computational resources without improving accuracy, particularly on simpler tasks.
A systematic framework for jointly evaluating both the efficiency and the reliability of reasoning processes in LRMs across diverse, multidisciplinary contexts was lacking.
results: Models like Qwen3-235b-a22b and Qwq-plus showed efficiency scores around 44-46% due to high token consumption (over 4,900 tokens average) and extensive reflection, despite achieving top-tier CoT quality (e.g., Qwen3-235b-a22b: 86.97% Precision, 85.80% Recall).

Grok-3-mini-beta achieved the highest reasoning efficiency at 61.69%, using significantly fewer tokens (1,891 average) while maintaining strong CoT quality, indicating more concise and effective inference.

Efficiency generally decreased for simple questions compared to difficult ones, reinforcing the overthinking hypothesis for easy tasks, while CoT quality metrics (reflection quality, recall, precision) typically declined slightly with increased task difficulty.
solution: The authors curated Think-Bench, a high-quality, multidisciplinary dataset comprising 1,375 annotated problems across mathematics, physics, and chemistry, with meticulously labeled key reasoning steps.

A comprehensive evaluation strategy was developed, introducing six new metrics to quantify thinking efficiency (e.g., Tokens, First Correct Tokens, Efficiency, Reflection Quality, Reflection Tokens, Thought Num).

A reference-based framework was adopted to evaluate Chain-of-Thought (CoT) quality, measuring Recall and Precision of generated reasoning steps against annotated references, using Claude 3.7 Sonnet as a judging model.
paper_summary.summary: This research introduces THINK-Bench, a multidisciplinary benchmark and evaluation framework designed to assess the reasoning efficiency and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) quality of large reasoning models. The study reveals that most models exhibit “overthinking” behavior, especially on simple tasks, leading to computational waste, and demonstrates a consistent trade-off between CoT quality and reasoning efficiency.

title: THINK-Bench: Evaluating Thinking Efficiency and Chain-of-Thought Quality  of Large Reasoning Models

abstract: By augmenting Large Language Models (LLMs) with external tools, their capacity to solve complex problems has been significantly enhanced. However, despite ongoing advancements in the parsing capabilities of LLMs, incorporating all available tools simultaneously in the prompt remains impractical due to the vast number of external tools. Consequently, it is essential to provide LLMs with a precise set of tools tailored to the specific task, considering both quantity and quality. Current tool retrieval methods primarily focus on refining the ranking list of tools and directly packaging a fixed number of top-ranked tools as the tool set. However, these approaches often fail to equip LLMs with the optimal set of tools prior to execution, since the optimal number of tools for different tasks could be different, resulting in inefficiencies such as redundant or unsuitable tools, which impede immediate access to the most relevant tools. This paper addresses the challenge of recommending precise toolsets for LLMs. We introduce the problem of tool recommendation, define its scope, and propose a novel Precision-driven Tool Recommendation (PTR) approach. PTR captures an initial, concise set of tools by leveraging historical tool bundle usage and dynamically adjusts the tool set by performing tool matching, culminating in a multi-view-based tool addition. Additionally, we present a new dataset, RecTools, and a metric, TRACC, designed to evaluate the effectiveness of tool recommendation for LLMs. We further validate our design choices through comprehensive experiments, demonstrating promising accuracy across two open benchmarks and our RecTools dataset.
title: Task-Aligned Tool Recommendation for Large Language Models

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are very costly and inefficient to update with new information. To address this limitation, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) has been proposed as a solution that dynamically incorporates external knowledge during inference, improving factual consistency and reducing hallucinations. Despite its promise, RAG systems face practical challenges-most notably, a strong dependence on the quality of the input query for accurate retrieval. In this paper, we investigate the sensitivity of different components in the RAG pipeline to various types of query perturbations. Our analysis reveals that the performance of commonly used retrievers can degrade significantly even under minor query variations. We study each module in isolation as well as their combined effect in an end-to-end question answering setting, using both general-domain and domain-specific datasets. Additionally, we propose an evaluation framework to systematically assess the query-level robustness of RAG pipelines and offer actionable recommendations for practitioners based on the results of more than 1092 experiments we performed.

keyInsights: Dense retrievers are more robust to redundant query information, while sparse retrievers show greater resilience to typographical errors.
LLM generators exhibit varied robustness depending on the dataset and the specific model, with ambiguity consistently degrading performance in both closed-book and oracle settings.

In general-domain contexts (NQ), end-to-end RAG robustness is largely dictated by the retriever's performance, but in domain-specific settings (BioASQ), the generator's ability to utilize context despite query perturbations becomes more critical.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle with factual accuracy (hallucination), costly updates, and lack of transparency when deployed in specialized domains.

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, while addressing these LLM limitations, exhibit unexamined sensitivity to variations in user query phrasing, typographical errors, or extraneous information.
Existing RAG robustness research often focuses on internal noise or specific module issues, lacking a comprehensive, query-level analysis across the entire end-to-end pipeline.
results: Typographical errors (25% level) consistently led to the lowest retriever performance across all configurations, while formal tone changes had the least impact.
LLMs in the oracle setting (with correct documents) largely recovered from 10% typo perturbations, indicating that high-quality retrieval can mitigate surface-level query errors.
Internal LLM representation analysis showed that redundancy and ambiguity in queries led to more scattered embeddings compared to original queries, even with perfect context provided.
solution: A systematic framework is introduced to measure RAG system robustness to query perturbations, enabling the decoupling of sensitivities in both the retrieval and generation modules.
Queries are perturbed using two main methods: LLM-generated semantic variations (redundancy, formal tone, ambiguity) and character-level typo insertions (10% and 25% of words).
Extensive experiments are conducted across four retrievers (dense and sparse), three 7-8B parameter LLM generators, and three datasets (general-domain NQ, HotpotQA, and domain-specific BioASQ).
paper_summary.summary: This research from TUM, Intel Labs, and Thoughtworks systematically investigates how retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems respond to various query-level perturbations, such as typos and semantic variations. The study introduces a framework to decouple the sensitivity of retrievers and generators, demonstrating module-specific robustness profiles across diverse datasets and model architectures.

title: Investigating the Robustness of Retrieval-Augmented Generation at the Query Level

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs), such as OpenAI-o1 and DeepSeek-R1, have demonstrated strong reasoning capabilities. To further enhance LLM capabilities, recent agentic systems, such as Deep Research, incorporate web interactions into LLM reasoning to mitigate uncertainties and reduce potential errors. However, existing research predominantly focuses on reasoning performance, often neglecting the efficiency of agentic systems. In this work, we present a comprehensive empirical study that identifies efficiency bottlenecks in web-interactive agentic systems. We decompose end-to-end latency into two primary components: LLM API latency and web environment latency. We conduct a comprehensive empirical study across 15 models and 5 providers to demonstrate high variability in API-based agentic systems. We observe that web environment latency can contribute as much as 53.7% to the overall latency in a web-based agentic system. To improve latency, we propose SpecCache, a caching framework augmented with speculative execution that can reduce web environment overhead. Extensive evaluations on two standard benchmarks show that our approach improves the cache hit rate by up to 58x compared to a random caching strategy, while reducing web environment overhead by up to 3.2x, without degrading agentic system performance.

keyInsights: Both LLM API latency and web environment latency contribute substantially to the end-to-end latency of agentic systems, with web interactions accounting for up to 53.7% of total time.
LLM API response times demonstrate high variability (up to 69.21x difference) across providers, models, times, and locations, highlighting a major challenge for consistent performance.
Speculative execution, traditionally used for token generation, can be generalized to agent actions to effectively hide environment interaction latency, even with a simpler draft model.
originalProblem: Agentic systems built on Large Language Models (LLMs) suffer from considerable end-to-end latency, hindering their deployment in real-world, latency-sensitive applications.
Existing LLM optimization methods primarily target internal model inference, neglecting the significant and highly variable delays introduced by external LLM APIs and web environment interactions.
The large and dynamic nature of web environments, with numerous clickable elements, makes traditional caching strategies for reducing interaction latency largely ineffective.
results: SpecCache reduced web environment overhead by up to 3.2x on the WebWalkerQA and Frames benchmarks.
The framework achieved a web action cache hit rate of up to 83.3% on WebWalkerQA and 54.0% on Frames, representing a substantial improvement over random caching strategies.
These efficiency gains were achieved without any degradation in the agentic system's reasoning performance or task success rate.
solution: Conducted a comprehensive empirical study to identify and quantify latency bottlenecks, decomposing end-to-end latency into LLM API and web environment components.
Introduced SpecCache, a novel caching framework that employs a smaller, asynchronous 'draft model' for speculative action prediction.
Asynchronously prefetched the outcomes of these predicted actions into an action-observation cache, overlapping environment interaction costs with the target LLM's reasoning process.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the University of Wisconsin-Madison, University of Toronto, and NVIDIA empirically analyzed the efficiency bottlenecks in web-interactive agentic Large Language Model (LLM) systems, identifying substantial latency from both LLM APIs and web environment interactions. They proposed SpecCache, a speculative caching framework that reduces web environment overhead by up to 3.2x and improves cache hit rates by up to 58x compared to random caching, without compromising task success.

title: What Limits Agentic Systems Efficiency?

abstract: The ability of Large Language Models (LLMs) to use external tools unlocks powerful real-world interactions, making rigorous evaluation essential. However, current benchmarks primarily report final accuracy, revealing what models can do but obscuring the cognitive bottlenecks that define their true capability boundaries. To move from simple performance scoring to a diagnostic tool, we introduce a framework grounded in Cognitive Load Theory. Our framework deconstructs task complexity into two quantifiable components: Intrinsic Load, the inherent structural complexity of the solution path, formalized with a novel Tool Interaction Graph; and Extraneous Load, the difficulty arising from ambiguous task presentation. To enable controlled experiments, we construct ToolLoad-Bench, the first benchmark with parametrically adjustable cognitive load. Our evaluation reveals distinct performance cliffs as cognitive load increases, allowing us to precisely map each model's capability boundary. We validate that our framework's predictions are highly calibrated with empirical results, establishing a principled methodology for understanding an agent's limits and a practical foundation for building more efficient systems.

keyInsights: Cognitive Load Theory can be directly applied as a formal, quantitative framework to deconstruct task complexity for AI agents, providing a diagnostic lens beyond simple accuracy.

The total cognitive load (CL_Total) exhibits an exponential relationship with task success probability, allowing for the quantitative mapping of model-specific capability boundaries (baseline capability and load sensitivity).

Understanding these cognitive profiles enables intelligent task routing in production systems, allowing dynamic allocation of tasks to different LLMs based on their specific capabilities and cost-efficiency.
originalProblem: Current LLM tool-use evaluation primarily relies on final accuracy, providing black-box results that do not explain *why* models succeed or fail.
There is a lack of understanding regarding the specific cognitive bottlenecks and operational limits that define an LLM agent's true capability boundaries.
Existing benchmarks treat task difficulty as a monolithic variable, lacking a granular framework to diagnose specific failure modes under varying cognitive demands.
results: ToolLoad-Bench proved challenging, with the specialized xLAM2-32B model achieving the highest accuracy at 78.8%, outperforming larger general-purpose models like GPT-4o (68.0%).
Both Intrinsic Load (structural complexity) and Extraneous Load (query ambiguity/distractors) consistently led to performance degradation across all evaluated models.

The framework's Load-Success Relationship was validated through strong statistical goodness-of-fit tests (e.g., Hosmer-Lemeshow p-values > 0.05 for all models), demonstrating high calibration between predicted probabilities and observed accuracy.
solution: A formal cognitive load framework was proposed, adapting Cognitive Load Theory to LLMs by deconstructing task complexity into quantifiable Intrinsic Load (CL_I) and Extraneous Load (CL_E).

CL_I is measured using Tool Interaction Graphs (TIG) that quantify memory load (attentional distance) and selection load (interference), while CL_E is measured by LLM-generated scores for query ambiguity and distractor tools.

The ToolLoad-Bench was constructed as the first benchmark with parametrically adjustable cognitive load, featuring 500 instances designed for controlled experimentation to systematically probe model limits.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the Chinese Academy of Sciences introduced a cognitive load framework to diagnose the capability boundaries of tool-use large language models. This framework quantifies task complexity using Intrinsic and Extraneous Loads, successfully mapping distinct performance profiles and operational limits for leading models, with xLAM2-32B achieving 78.8% accuracy on the new ToolLoad-Bench.

title: Beyond Accuracy: A Cognitive Load Framework for Mapping the Capability Boundaries of Tool-use Agents

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in using external tools to address user inquiries. However, most existing evaluations assume tool use in short contexts, offering limited insight into model behavior during realistic long-term interactions. To fill this gap, we introduce ToolHaystack, a benchmark for testing the tool use capabilities in long-term interactions. Each test instance in ToolHaystack includes multiple tasks execution contexts and realistic noise within a continuous conversation, enabling assessment of how well models maintain context and handle various disruptions. By applying this benchmark to 14 state-of-the-art LLMs, we find that while current models perform well in standard multi-turn settings, they often significantly struggle in ToolHaystack, highlighting critical gaps in their long-term robustness not revealed by previous tool benchmarks.
title: ToolHaystack: Stress-Testing Tool-Augmented Language Models in Realistic Long-Term Interactions

abstract: Perplexity is a widely adopted metric for assessing the predictive quality of large language models (LLMs) and often serves as a reference metric for downstream evaluations. However, recent evidence shows that perplexity can be unreliable, especially when irrelevant long inputs are used, raising concerns for both benchmarking and system deployment. While prior efforts have employed selective input filtering and curated datasets, the impact of input length on perplexity has not been systematically studied from a systems perspective and input length has rarely been treated as a first-class system variable affecting both fairness and efficiency. In this work, we close this gap by introducing LengthBenchmark, a system-conscious evaluation framework that explicitly integrates input length, evaluation protocol design, and system-level costs, evaluating representative LLMs under two scoring protocols (direct accumulation and fixed window sliding) across varying context lengths. Unlike prior work that focuses solely on accuracy-oriented metrics, LengthBenchmark additionally measures latency, memory footprint, and evaluation cost, thereby linking predictive metrics to deployment realities. We further incorporate quantized variants not as a main contribution, but as robustness checks, showing that length-induced biases persist across both full-precision and compressed models. This design disentangles the effects of evaluation logic, quantization, and input length, and demonstrates that length bias is a general phenomenon that undermines fair cross-model comparison. Our analysis yields two key observations: (i) sliding window evaluation consistently inflates performance on short inputs, and (ii) both full-precision and quantized models appear to realise gains as the evaluated segment length grows.
title: Rethinking Perplexity: Revealing the Impact of Input Length on Perplexity Evaluation in LLMs

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) such as GPT-5 and Gemini 3 have pushed the frontier of automated reasoning and code generation. Yet current benchmarks emphasize accuracy and output quality, neglecting a critical dimension: efficiency of token usage. The token efficiency is highly variable in practical. Models solving the same problem with similar accuracy can exhibit up to a \textbf{5.0$\times$} difference in token length, leading to massive gap of model reasoning ability. Such variance exposes significant redundancy, highlighting the critical need for a standardized benchmark to quantify the gap of token efficiency. Thus, we introduce OckBench, the first benchmark that jointly measures accuracy and token efficiency across reasoning and coding tasks. Our evaluation reveals that token efficiency remains largely unoptimized across current models, significantly inflating serving costs and latency. These findings provide a concrete roadmap for the community to optimize the latent reasoning ability, token efficiency. Ultimately, we argue for an evaluation paradigm shift: tokens must not be multiplied beyond necessity. Our benchmarks are available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Decoding token count is a crucial, yet overlooked, dimension for assessing the practical efficiency of LLMs, independent of hardware or system configurations.
Significant variance in token consumption exists among LLMs, even when their accuracy levels are comparable, indicating token efficiency as a key differentiator.
The research empirically demonstrates an accuracy-efficiency trade-off, revealing that token-intensive reasoning strategies do not always yield proportionally higher accuracy.
originalProblem: Existing LLM evaluation benchmarks predominantly focus on accuracy and output quality, largely overlooking the computational efficiency with which results are achieved.
Advanced reasoning strategies like Chain of Thought (CoT) lead to substantial increases in decoding token count, incurring significant latency, energy, and operational costs.
Current efficiency-oriented frameworks are system- or hardware-level, failing to quantify the intrinsic reasoning efficiency of the LLM itself in a model-agnostic way.
results: Commercial models achieved higher average accuracy (60.8% on OckBench-Math) but exhibited wide variance in token efficiency; GPT-4o consistently emerged as the most token-efficient commercial model.

Open-source models' 'thinking' variants produced substantially higher token counts (e.g., Qwen3-14B 'thinking' used 8,190 tokens for 40% math accuracy vs. 3,010 tokens for 33% by 'non-thinking'), often without proportional accuracy gains.

Some open-source models, like NovaSky-AI Sky-T1-7B, achieved token efficiency comparable to efficient commercial models (556 tokens for 33% math accuracy), challenging assumptions that smaller models are always inherently more efficient.
solution: Develops OckBench, a unified benchmark that jointly evaluates LLM reasoning by measuring both decoding token consumption and task accuracy.
Formalizes decoding token count as an intrinsic, model- and hardware-agnostic efficiency metric to complement traditional accuracy assessments.

Utilizes curated questions from mathematics (GSM8K, AIME24, AIME25) and software engineering (MBPP + real-world problems), selected for their high variance in decoding token usage among baseline models.
paper_summary.summary: OckBench introduces a new evaluation paradigm for Large Language Models (LLMs) by jointly measuring decoding token efficiency and task accuracy, addressing the rising computational cost of advanced reasoning. The benchmark revealed significant variability in token consumption across models with similar accuracy, highlighting a critical trade-off between performance and resource use.

title: OckBench: Measuring the Efficiency of LLM Reasoning

abstract:
Existing evaluations of tool learning primarily focus on validating the
alignment of selected tools for large language models (LLMs) with expected
outcomes. However, these approaches rely on a limited set of scenarios where
answers can be pre-determined, diverging from genuine needs. Furthermore, a
sole emphasis on outcomes disregards the complex capabilities required for LLMs
to effectively use tools. To tackle this issue, we propose ToolEyes, a
fine-grained system tailored for the evaluation of the LLMs' tool learning
capabilities in authentic scenarios. The system meticulously examines seven
real-world scenarios, analyzing five dimensions crucial to LLMs in tool
learning: format alignment, intent comprehension, behavior planning, tool
selection, and answer organization. Additionally, ToolEyes incorporates a tool
library boasting approximately 600 tools, serving as an intermediary between
LLMs and the physical world. Evaluations involving ten LLMs across three
categories reveal a preference for specific scenarios and limited cognitive
abilities in tool learning. Intriguingly, expanding the model size even
exacerbates the hindrance to tool learning. The code and data are available at
this https URL

keyInsights: Behavioral planning is a significant limitation across all evaluated LLMs, including GPT-4 (35.70% proficiency), highlighting a critical gap in their cognitive processes for multi-step tasks.

Counter-intuitively, increasing model size (within LLaMA-2-chat and Vicuna-1.5 families) can weaken instrumental learning due to amplified undesirable behaviors like redundant sentences or tool selection hallucinations.
A strong positive correlation exists among the five capability dimensions (mostly above 0.7), suggesting that tool learning is a synergistic process where improving one aspect often benefits others.
originalProblem: Existing LLM evaluation methods are outcome-centric, neglecting the complex cognitive processes (e.g., intent comprehension, planning) involved in tool utilization.
Current benchmarks are limited to narrow, predefined scenarios, failing to represent the dynamic and diverse nature of real-world tool application and information variability.
A lack of fine-grained, process-oriented analysis prevents understanding *how* LLMs effectively use tools, hindering targeted improvements and capability diagnosis.
results: Closed-source LLMs, particularly GPT-4, significantly outperform open-source models in overall tool learning, with GPT-4 achieving 70.31% accuracy compared to ToolLLaMA-2-7B-v2's 56.30%.

LLMs demonstrate scenario-specific performance, excelling in 'Text Generation' and 'Data Understanding' but struggling in 'Information Retrieval' and 'Financial Transactions' where output synthesis is crucial.
Higher performing models exhibit greater efficiency, completing tasks in fewer interaction turns (e.g., GPT-4 averaged 2.8 turns versus LLaMA-2-chat-7B's 7.0 turns).
solution: ToolEyes establishes a comprehensive benchmark with seven real-world scenarios and a curated library of 568 tools, supported by 382 human-validated user queries.
It evaluates LLMs across five fine-grained capabilities: Format Alignment, Intent Comprehension, Behavior Planning, Tool Selection, and Answer Organization.
The system employs both rule-based metrics and GPT-4 for qualitative assessments of LLM responses, ensuring a diagnostic and process-oriented evaluation framework.
paper_summary.summary: ToolEyes presents a fine-grained evaluation system for assessing large language models' tool learning capabilities across diverse real-world scenarios. It identifies behavioral planning as a critical bottleneck and demonstrates that increasing model size can sometimes hinder tool use due to amplified undesirable behaviors.

title: ToolEyes: Fine-Grained Evaluation for Tool Learning Capabilities of  Large Language Models in Real-world Scenarios

abstract:
Efficiently deploying large language models (LLMs) in real-world scenarios
remains a critical challenge, primarily due to hardware heterogeneity,
inference framework limitations, and workload complexities.Efficiently
deploying large language models (LLMs) in real-world scenarios remains a
critical challenge, primarily due to hardware heterogeneity, inference
framework limitations, and workload complexities. These challenges often lead
to inefficiencies in memory utilization, latency, and throughput, hindering the
effective deployment of LLMs, especially for non-experts. Through extensive
experiments, we identify key performance bottlenecks, including sudden drops in
memory utilization, latency fluctuations with varying batch sizes, and
inefficiencies in multi-GPU configurations. These insights reveal a vast
optimization space shaped by the intricate interplay of hardware, frameworks,
and workload parameters. This underscores the need for a systematic approach to
optimize LLM inference, motivating the design of our framework, GUIDE. GUIDE
leverages dynamic modeling and simulation-based optimization to address these
issues, achieving prediction errors between 9.9% and 42.3% for key metrics such
as batch latency, TTFT, and decode throughput. By effectively bridging the gap
between theoretical performance and practical deployment, our framework
empowers practitioners, particularly non-specialists, to make data-driven
decisions and unlock the full potential of LLMs in heterogeneous environments
cheaply.

keyInsights: LLM inference performance is highly sensitive to the dynamic and complex interactions between specific hardware, chosen inference frameworks, and workload parameters.
Modeling memory utilization, particularly KV-cache behavior, and computational costs specific to inference frameworks like vLLM and FastGen, is essential for accurate performance prediction.
Optimizing multi-GPU deployment necessitates a comprehensive simulation of hybrid parallelism, accounting for compute, memory bandwidth, and communication overheads to identify bottlenecks.
originalProblem: Efficiently deploying Large Language Models in heterogeneous environments is challenging due to immense model sizes, high computational demands, and strict performance requirements.

Current optimization methods are often fragmented, failing to holistically address the complex interplay between model architectures, diverse hardware, various inference frameworks, and dynamic workloads.
Manual tuning of LLM deployment is resource-intensive and leads to suboptimal performance, characterized by memory utilization drops, inconsistent latency, and inefficiencies in multi-GPU setups.
results: On single-GPU configurations, GUIDE achieved prediction errors for vLLM ranging from 9.9% for TTFT to 33.04% for batch latency, and for FastGen from 12.30% for TTFT to 32.74% for batch latency.

In multi-GPU setups, vLLM's batch latency prediction error decreased to 25.19%, while TTFT errors for both vLLM and FastGen significantly increased to 30.40% and 42.30% respectively, indicating challenges in modeling inter-GPU communication.

The system's prediction accuracy for key performance metrics generally ranged from 9.9% to 42.3%, highlighting a practical step towards data-driven LLM deployment despite modeling complexities, especially with high-performance GPUs like A100.
solution: The GUIDE system integrates an Inference Engine, Model Analyzer, and Parallel Simulator to provide a unified framework for LLM deployment optimization.
It models the entire LLM inference process, systematically exploring a multi-dimensional parameter space encompassing hardware, models, frameworks, and workload parameters.
GUIDE automates the configuration of optimal batch sizes, GPU selection, and parallel strategies by predicting performance metrics and simulating task execution in heterogeneous environments.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Sun Yat-sen University developed GUIDE, a unified inference engine designed to optimize the deployment of Large Language Models in diverse hardware and software environments. This system systematically models and simulates LLM inference to predict performance metrics like batch latency and throughput, facilitating data-driven configuration decisions.

title: GUIDE: A Global Unified Inference Engine for Deploying Large Language  Models in Heterogeneous Environments

abstract: The rapid evolution of Large Language Model (LLM) inference systems has yielded significant efficiency improvements. However, our systematic analysis reveals that current evaluation methodologies frequently exhibit fundamental flaws, often manifesting as common evaluation anti-patterns that obscure true performance characteristics and impede scientific progress. Through a comprehensive examination of recent systems, we identify recurring anti-patterns across three key dimensions: Baseline Fairness, Evaluation Setup, and Metric Design. These anti-patterns are uniquely problematic for LLM inference due to its dual-phase nature combining distinct prefill and decode operations, its handling of highly heterogeneous workloads, and its strict temporal requirements for interactive use. We demonstrate how common anti-patterns -- such as inadequate baseline comparisons that conflate engineering effort with algorithmic novelty, workload selections that fail to represent production scenarios, and metric normalizations that hide substantial performance variability like generation stalls-lead to misleading conclusions. To address these challenges, we provide a comprehensive checklist derived from our analysis, establishing a framework for recognizing and avoiding these anti-patterns in favor of robust LLM inference evaluation. To demonstrate the practical application of our framework, we present a case study analyzing speculative decoding, a technique whose bursty, non-uniform token generation is easily misinterpreted when evaluated using approaches characteristic of these anti-patterns. Our work establishes a rigorous foundation for evaluation methodology, enabling meaningful comparisons, ensuring reproducible results, and ultimately accelerating genuine progress in LLM inference systems by moving beyond common anti-patterns to align evaluation with real-world requirements.

keyInsights: Eight recurring "anti-patterns" significantly distort performance reporting in LLM inference, including conflating implementation with algorithms, using outdated models, and ignoring practical latency bounds.

Solely relying on summary statistics or normalized metrics can obscure crucial performance distributions, tail latencies, and user-perceived issues like generation stalls or high Time To First Token (TTFT).

Rigorous evaluation practices, considering diverse workloads and complete performance distributions, are essential for accurately understanding system behavior and ensuring advancements translate to real-world utility.
originalProblem: Rapid innovation in LLM inference systems has outpaced evaluation methodologies, leading to inconsistent and often misleading performance claims.
Current evaluations frequently fail to account for the unique complexities of LLM inference, such as its dual-phase nature, heterogeneous workloads, and strict temporal requirements.
Inadequate evaluation practices hinder accurate comparison between systems, impede scientific progress, and obscure real-world system behavior and user experience.
results: The study identifies eight specific anti-patterns, such as "Conflating Implementation and Algorithm" and "Ignoring Practical Latency Bounds," which are prevalent in state-of-the-art LLM inference papers.
